heading: Assessment
html:
Assess abnormal liver blood tests in clinical context, not as isolated numbers. Classify biochemical pattern, identify red flags, and assess synthetic function early.

  • Take focused history: alcohol, medicines/supplements, viral and metabolic risk factors, and symptom trajectory.
  • Examine for jaundice, hepatomegaly, chronic liver disease stigmata, ascites, and encephalopathy.
  • Interpret pattern as hepatocellular, cholestatic, or mixed and compare with prior results/trend.
  • Include synthetic markers and full blood count/platelets in baseline assessment where clinically indicated.

heading: Primary care management
html:
The default approach is red-flag first triage, followed by pattern-directed investigation and time-bounded review.

  • Mild abnormalities (for example less than about 2 times ULN in clinically stable patients): repeat tests, review reversible causes, and reassess.
  • More significant derangement (for example above about 2 to 3 times ULN): investigate promptly and consider early specialist input.
  • Address reversible drivers early, including alcohol reduction/abstinence where relevant and medication review for possible drug-induced injury.
  • Differentiate cholestatic pattern from non-hepatic ALP causes when ALP is isolated (for example consider vitamin D/bone context).
  • Use clear safety-netting: earlier review for symptom progression, worsening bloods, or new synthetic dysfunction features.

heading: Who to refer
html:
Urgent same-day referral

  • Jaundice with low albumin or prolonged INR/raised PT suggesting synthetic failure.
  • Ascites, encephalopathy, active bleeding, or other decompensation features.
  • Marked cholestasis with concerning systemic features (for example weight loss) suggesting possible obstructive or malignant pathology.
  • Marked transaminase derangement or rapid biochemical deterioration.

Routine referral

  • Persistent unexplained abnormal liver tests despite appropriate primary care reassessment.
  • Suspected chronic liver disease requiring specialist staging and long-term management planning.

Include in referral

  • Trend of liver blood tests and synthetic markers.
  • Medication/alcohol history and actions already taken.
  • Associated symptoms, examination findings, and comorbidity profile.

heading: Who not to refer
html:
Do not routinely refer asymptomatic patients with mild, likely reversible abnormalities at first presentation when there are no red flags, no synthetic dysfunction, and a safe short-interval review plan is in place.

Continue primary care follow-up with repeat testing and escalation if abnormalities persist or clinical status worsens.

html:

  • BSG: Guidelines on the management of abnormal liver blood tests
  • NENC Hepatology Network: Abnormal liver blood test guideline
